mainEntity.name: Why does my daughter is three and a half. she can say some words – maybe twenty – but she can't use them to ask for things. she might say 'juice' when she sees juice, but she can't say 'juice' to ask for juice when she's thirsty. the words she has are labels, not tools. when she wants something, she cries. when she needs something, she screams. when she's desperate, she bites herself. her speech therapist talks about 'manding' and 'functional communication' but what i see is a child who can't tell me she's hungry, can't ask for her blanket, can't request to go to the park. i've tried picture cards – she throws them. i've tried sign language – she won't imitate. i've tried waiting for her to use words – the meltdowns come first. every meal is a crisis because she can't tell me what she wants to eat. every bedtime is a battle because she can't tell me what's wrong. i'm exhausted from guessing, she's exhausted from not being understood, and our whole family is living in constant tension. what actually works to teach requesting to a child who has the wants but not the way to express them?

acceptedAnswer.text: MANDING / REQUESTING / FUNCTIONAL COMMUNICATION TRAINING / VERBAL OPERANT DEVELOPMENT! Requesting (technically called 'manding' in verbal behavior terminology) is communication that is controlled by motivation – the child communicates because they want or need something. It's the most functional and powerful form of early communication because it directly benefits the child. Unlike labeling (tacting) where the child names what they see, or echoing (echoics) where the child repeats what they hear, requesting comes from internal motivation – hunger, thirst, desire for objects or activities, need for comfort. Teaching requesting gives children power over their environment and dramatically reduces frustration-driven behaviors. Materials for teaching requesting must capture motivation, provide accessible communication methods, ensure immediate reinforcement, and build toward spontaneous, independent requests.

mainEntity.name: What is Manding / Requesting / Functional Communication Training / Verbal Operant Development?

acceptedAnswer.text: Requesting (technically called 'manding' in verbal behavior terminology) is communication that is controlled by motivation – the child communicates because they want or need something. It's the most functional and powerful form of early communication because it directly benefits the child. Unlike labeling (tacting) where the child names what they see, or echoing (echoics) where the child repeats what they hear, requesting comes from internal motivation – hunger, thirst, desire for objects or activities, need for comfort. Teaching requesting gives children power over their environment and dramatically reduces frustration-driven behaviors. Materials for teaching requesting must capture motivation, provide accessible communication methods, ensure immediate reinforcement, and build toward spontaneous, independent requests. Common signs include: .

mainEntity.name: How does a High-Motivation Item Collection help with manding?

acceptedAnswer.text: You can't teach requesting without something worth requesting. Before any communication method matters, you need items your child wants intensely enough to work for. This isn't about educational toys or things you think they should want – it's about what actually motivates YOUR child. For some children, it's a specific food (the only thing they'll eat). For others, it's a particular sensory experience (spinning, bouncing, bubbles). For others, it's technology, specific toys, or activities. The collection should include items at different motivation levels: some they'll always want, some they want sometimes, and some that can build new motivation. These become the 'reinforcers' that make requesting powerful – the child learns that communication = getting what they want. Price: ₹200–2,000 depending on child's interests. DIY option: Building a high-motivation collection: DISCOVERY PROCESS: Observe what your child: Reaches for repeatedly, Gets upset when removed, Chooses when given options, Shows excitement about, Will work or wait for, Protests when they can't have. Ask family members what they've noticed. Try novel items – sometimes new things create motivation. CATEGORIZE BY MOTIVATION LEVEL: Tier 1 (Highest): Items child ALWAYS wants, will do anything for. These are for teaching new skills. Tier 2 (High): Items child usually wants, strong motivation. These are for practicing and maintaining skills. Tier 3 (Moderate): Items child sometimes wants. These are for expanding request vocabulary. TYPES OF MOTIVATORS: Edibles: Specific foods, drinks, treats (small pieces for multiple trials). Sensory: Bubbles, spinning toys, vibrating items, music, lights, textures. Activities: Tickles, being swung, iPad time, going outside, car rides. Objects: Specific toys, comfort items, books, videos. Social: Certain games, interactions, attention from specific people. CRITICAL: CONTROL ACCESS: Motivation comes from want, want comes from not having. Items used for requesting teaching should NOT be freely available. They should be visible but out of reach, or available only through communication. This is not deprivation – it's creating communication opportunities. PREFERENCE ASSESSMENT: Regularly reassess what motivates. Preferences change. Today's high motivator may bore them next week. Keep discovering new items. QUANTITY: Have many options. You'll need multiple items for repeated teaching trials without satiation (child getting bored of item). Safety: Ensure all items are safe for your child's developmental level. Food items should be appropriate for age and any dietary needs. Controlling access is about creating communication opportunity, not deprivation of basic needs.

mainEntity.name: How does a First Requests Visual Communication Board help with manding?

acceptedAnswer.text: For children who don't yet use words to request, a visual communication board provides an alternative way to communicate wants. The board starts simple – maybe just 2-4 pictures of the child's highest-motivation items. The child learns to touch or give a picture to get the item. Unlike speech, pictures don't require motor planning for sound production. Unlike pointing, pictures are more specific and clear. The board is a bridge – some children will move from pictures to words, others will develop sophisticated visual communication systems. The key is starting with items the child desperately wants and making the picture-to-item connection immediately clear. Touch the picture, get the thing. This is the foundation of all picture-based communication. Price: ₹100–500 or Free (homemade). DIY option: Creating first requests board: START SMALL: 2-4 pictures maximum initially. Only items from Tier 1 motivation list. Larger board comes later – start with success. PICTURE QUALITY: Real photos of actual items work best initially. Clear, uncluttered background. Item should fill most of the picture. Consider photos of YOUR items, not generic images. Can use clear, simple drawings once photos are established. BOARD FORMAT: Velcro board with removable pictures, OR Laminated board with permanent pictures. Size: Large enough to see and touch easily. Portable option for different locations. PICTURE SIZE: Larger for younger children or those with motor challenges. 2-3 inches is typical starting size. Can reduce size as skills develop. PLACEMENT: Where requests naturally occur: Kitchen for food requests, Play area for toy requests, Throughout house as skills grow. At child's eye level and within reach. TEACHING SEQUENCE: Step 1: Physical guidance – Hand-over-hand to touch picture, immediately give item. Step 2: Reduce prompting – Fade to pointing toward picture, then just having board present. Step 3: Spontaneous – Child initiates going to board and touching picture. Step 4: Expand – Add more pictures as first requests are solid. CRITICAL ELEMENTS: IMMEDIATE reinforcement – as soon as they touch, give item. MOTIVATED items only – if they don't want it, they won't request it. CONSISTENT response – every touch should result in item (at first). Safety: Pictures should be laminated or durable enough for repeated handling. Ensure board is stable and won't fall. Some children may try to eat pictures – monitor and use appropriate materials.

mainEntity.name: How does a Single-Message Voice Output Devices help with manding?

acceptedAnswer.text: For some children, activating a button that 'speaks' for them is more motivating and effective than pointing to pictures. Single-message devices (like Big Mack, Little Mack, or similar) play one recorded message when pressed. You record 'I want bubbles' on a button and place it near the bubbles. When your child presses it, they hear the request AND get the bubbles. The auditory feedback helps connect the action to communication. The device speaks even though the child can't yet. Over time, the child learns that pressing the button = getting what they want. This is particularly powerful for children who are motivated by technology, cause-effect relationships, or auditory feedback. Single-message devices are simpler than full AAC systems – perfect for teaching that 'pressing something = communication.' Price: ₹1,000–5,000 per device. DIY option: Using single-message voice output devices: DEVICE OPTIONS: Commercial devices: Big Mack, Little Mack, Step-by-Step (multiple messages), Cheap Talk. DIY option: Recordable buttons from electronics stores or online, Talking buttons/recordable cards. App-based: Single-button apps on tablet, but dedicated device often works better for focus. SETUP: Record clear request message: 'I want [item]' or just item name. Match message to what's available in that moment. Place device near the desired item (bubbles button near bubbles). TEACHING: Physical prompt initially – hand-over-hand to press button. Immediately provide item when button is pressed. Fade prompting over time. Goal: child independently goes to button and presses. MESSAGE TIPS: Record in voice child responds to (often parent's voice). Keep messages short and clear. Match language to child's current level. Can use just item name ('bubbles') or full request ('I want bubbles please'). EXPANDING: One button for one item initially. Add buttons for different items in different locations. Eventually move to multi-message devices or full AAC. MULTIPLE LOCATION STRATEGY: Bubbles button in living room near bubbles. Snack button in kitchen near snacks. Outside button near door. Each button = one clear request in one clear context. WHY IT WORKS: Low motor demand – just press. Auditory feedback is reinforcing. Clear cause-effect relationship. Technology is motivating for many children. Safety: Ensure devices are sturdy and safe for mouthing if child explores orally. Check batteries regularly. Supervise initially to ensure appropriate use.

mainEntity.name: How does a First Signs Visual Teaching Cards help with manding?

acceptedAnswer.text: Sign language can be a powerful requesting tool because it's always available – no device to find, no picture board needed, hands are always there. But learning signs requires imitation skills and motor planning that some children find challenging. First signs visual cards show simple signs for high-motivation items with clear photos and step-by-step hand positions. Cards for 'more,' 'eat,' 'drink,' 'help,' and specific preferred items give visual models for both child and caregivers to reference. The cards help caregivers learn the signs so they can model consistently, and provide visual supports for teaching. For children who can imitate motor movements, signs can become faster than pictures – hands are always available, always ready to communicate. Price: ₹200–800 or Free (printables available). DIY option: Creating and using first signs cards: SELECTING FIRST SIGNS: Choose signs for high-motivation items. Prioritize: 'more' (universal – works for any repeated item), 'eat/food,' 'drink,' 'help,' 'all done/finished,' Specific favorites: 'bubbles,' 'ball,' 'music,' etc. Start with 3-5 most functional signs. CARD FORMAT: Photo of the sign (final hand position). Sequence photos if sign has movement. Written description of how to make sign. Picture of item the sign represents. Consider video QR codes linking to sign demonstrations. SIGN MODIFICATIONS: Official ASL/ISL signs can be modified for motor abilities. 'Approximations' are acceptable – communication matters more than perfection. Consistent approximation within your family is fine. TEACHING APPROACH: Model the sign every time you give the item. Hand-over-hand prompting to form sign, then give item. Fade prompting progressively. Accept approximations and shape over time. FAMILY CONSISTENCY: Everyone in household should learn the signs. Post cards where relevant (kitchen, play area). Practice together so child sees signs frequently. ADVANTAGES OF SIGNS: Always available – no device to find. Fast once learned. Can combine with speech. Develops motor planning skills. CHALLENGES TO ANTICIPATE: Requires motor imitation ability. Some children struggle with fine motor control. Signs may not be understood by unfamiliar people. May work best as supplement to other systems. Safety: Accept and reinforce approximations of signs. Demanding perfection before reinforcement kills motivation. The goal is communication, not perfect signing.

mainEntity.name: How does a Request Communication Book (PECS-Style) help with manding?

acceptedAnswer.text: A request communication book is a portable system that grows with your child's communication. Unlike a simple board with a few pictures, a communication book can hold dozens or hundreds of request options organized by category, travel anywhere your child goes, and support increasingly complex communication. The book typically starts with a front page for active requesting, with pictures stored in categorized pages inside. The child takes a picture from inside the book, brings it to someone, and exchanges it for the item. This 'exchange' element teaches the social nature of communication – you communicate TO someone. As skills grow, the book expands to include more items, action words, and eventually sentence building. This is the foundation of PECS (Picture Exchange Communication System) and similar approaches. Price: ₹300–1,500. DIY option: Building a request communication book: BOOK STRUCTURE: Small binder or sturdy folder. Velcro strip on front cover (sentence strip area for later). Velcro strip inside front cover for 'active' pictures. Category pages inside: food, toys, activities, places, people. Pictures stored on category pages, moved to front for requesting. INITIAL SETUP: Start with just a few high-motivation pictures. Don't fill book with dozens of pictures initially. Add pictures as child masters existing ones. TEACHING EXCHANGE SEQUENCE (PECS Phases adapted): Phase 1: See item → Take picture → Give to partner → Get item (physical prompting, one picture at a time). Phase 2: Increase distance – child must travel to book and/or to communication partner. Phase 3: Picture discrimination – choose correct picture from multiple options. Phase 4: Sentence building – 'I want' + item picture. Phase 5: Responding to questions – 'What do you want?' Phase 6: Commenting – moves beyond requesting. EXCHANGE IS CRITICAL: Child must give picture TO someone. Not just touch it, not just point to it. The giving teaches communication is social – directed at another person. BOOK MANAGEMENT: Keep book accessible but organized. Child should know where book is kept. Take book everywhere – it's the child's voice. Regular inventory – add new interests, remove outgrown items. CATEGORY ORGANIZATION (as book grows): Foods/Drinks, Toys/Objects, Activities, Places, People, Feelings, Actions. Safety: The book is the child's communication system – treat it with importance. Don't let it get lost or damaged. Ensure child knows how to find it.

mainEntity.name: How does a Core Vocabulary Request Symbols help with manding?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most early requesting focuses on specific items – cookies, bubbles, iPad. But there's a set of 'core vocabulary' words that appear in almost all communication and are incredibly powerful for requesting: 'more,' 'want,' 'help,' 'stop,' 'go,' 'open,' 'give.' These core words combine with any item to create flexible communication. Instead of needing a picture for every specific item (which is impossible), the child learns 'want + [point to anything]' or 'more + [activity happening].' Core vocabulary symbols – whether as pictures, buttons on a device, or signs – give your child flexible communication power that transfers across items and situations. Teaching core words alongside specific item words creates a more powerful and flexible communication system. Price: ₹100–400 or Free (printables). DIY option: Creating and teaching core vocabulary symbols: ESSENTIAL FIRST CORE WORDS: 'more' – Request continuation of any preferred activity. 'want' – Express desire for any item or activity. 'help' – Request assistance with any challenge. 'stop' / 'all done' – End any non-preferred activity. 'go' – Request to leave, to start movement. 'open' – Request opening containers, doors, packages. SYMBOL FORMAT: Consistent symbols (choose one symbol set and use it). Larger size than item-specific pictures. Prominent placement on communication board/book/device. Color-coding can help distinguish from item words. TEACHING 'MORE': Most powerful first core word. Works with any repeatable activity: more tickles, more bubbles, more snack, more swinging. Teach by: Start preferred activity → Pause → Prompt 'more' → Continue activity. Once child uses 'more' spontaneously for one activity, transfer to others. TEACHING 'WANT': More specific than 'more' – indicates desire for item. Teach with visible desired items. 'Want' + point to item = get item. On device/board: 'want' button + item picture. TEACHING 'HELP': Introduce during tasks child can't do alone. Opening containers, reaching high items, fixing broken toys. 'Help' = immediate assistance. COMBINING CORE + SPECIFIC: 'More' + 'bubbles' = more specific request. 'Want' + 'cookie' = clear item request. 'Help' + 'open' = action request. This combination is early sentence building. PLACEMENT: Core words always visible and accessible. Don't hide them in book pages. Front of communication board/book, home page of device. Safety: Core words should be taught to empowerment, not compliance. 'Stop' and 'no' are important core words – children need ways to refuse and protest.

These are for teaching new skills. Tier 2 (High): Items child usually wants, strong motivation. These are for practicing and maintaining skills. Tier 3 (Moderate): Items child sometimes wants. These are for expanding request vocabulary. TYPES OF MOTIVATORS: Edibles: Specific foods, drinks, treats (small pieces for multiple trials). Sensory: Bubbles, spinning toys, vibrating items, music, lights, textures. Activities: Tickles, being swung, iPad time, going outside, car rides. Objects: Specific toys, comfort items, books, videos. Social: Certain games, interactions, attention from specific people. CRITICAL: CONTROL ACCESS: Motivation comes from want, want comes from not having. Items used for requesting teaching should NOT be freely available. They should be visible but out of reach, or available only through communication. This is not deprivation – it's creating communication opportunities. PREFERENCE ASSESSMENT: Regularly reassess what motivates. Preferences change. Today's high motivator may bore them next week. Keep discovering new items. QUANTITY: Have many options. You'll need multiple items for repeated teaching trials without satiation (child getting bored of item).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure all items are safe for your child's developmental level. Food items should be appropriate for age and any dietary needs. Controlling access is about creating communication opportunity, not deprivation of basic needs.

item.name: First Requests Visual Communication Board
item.description: For children who don't yet use words to request, a visual communication board provides an alternative way to communicate wants. The board starts simple – maybe just 2-4 pictures of the child's highest-motivation items. The child learns to touch or give a picture to get the item. Unlike speech, pictures don't require motor planning for sound production. Unlike pointing, pictures are more specific and clear. The board is a bridge – some children will move from pictures to words, others will develop sophisticated visual communication systems. The key is starting with items the child desperately wants and making the picture-to-item connection immediately clear. Touch the picture, get the thing. This is the foundation of all picture-based communication.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating first requests board: START SMALL: 2-4 pictures maximum initially. Only items from Tier 1 motivation list. Larger board comes later – start with success. PICTURE QUALITY: Real photos of actual items work best initially. Clear, uncluttered background. Item should fill most of the picture. Consider photos of YOUR items, not generic images. Can use clear, simple drawings once photos are established. BOARD FORMAT: Velcro board with removable pictures, OR Laminated board with permanent pictures. Size: Large enough to see and touch easily. Portable option for different locations. PICTURE SIZE: Larger for younger children or those with motor challenges. 2-3 inches is typical starting size. Can reduce size as skills develop. PLACEMENT: Where requests naturally occur: Kitchen for food requests, Play area for toy requests, Throughout house as skills grow. At child's eye level and within reach. TEACHING SEQUENCE: Step 1: Physical guidance – Hand-over-hand to touch picture, immediately give item. Step 2: Reduce prompting – Fade to pointing toward picture, then just having board present. Step 3: Spontaneous – Child initiates going to board and touching picture. Step 4: Expand – Add more pictures as first requests are solid. CRITICAL ELEMENTS: IMMEDIATE reinforcement – as soon as they touch, give item. MOTIVATED items only – if they don't want it, they won't request it. CONSISTENT response – every touch should result in item (at first).

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Pictures should be laminated or durable enough for repeated handling. Ensure board is stable and won't fall. Some children may try to eat pictures – monitor and use appropriate materials.

item.name: Single-Message Voice Output Devices
item.description: For some children, activating a button that 'speaks' for them is more motivating and effective than pointing to pictures. Single-message devices (like Big Mack, Little Mack, or similar) play one recorded message when pressed. You record 'I want bubbles' on a button and place it near the bubbles. When your child presses it, they hear the request AND get the bubbles. The auditory feedback helps connect the action to communication. The device speaks even though the child can't yet. Over time, the child learns that pressing the button = getting what they want. This is particularly powerful for children who are motivated by technology, cause-effect relationships, or auditory feedback. Single-message devices are simpler than full AAC systems – perfect for teaching that 'pressing something = communication.'

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Using single-message voice output devices: DEVICE OPTIONS: Commercial devices: Big Mack, Little Mack, Step-by-Step (multiple messages), Cheap Talk. DIY option: Recordable buttons from electronics stores or online, Talking buttons/recordable cards. App-based: Single-button apps on tablet, but dedicated device often works better for focus. SETUP: Record clear request message: 'I want [item]' or just item name. Match message to what's available in that moment. Place device near the desired item (bubbles button near bubbles). TEACHING: Physical prompt initially – hand-over-hand to press button. Immediately provide item when button is pressed. Fade prompting over time. Goal: child independently goes to button and presses. MESSAGE TIPS: Record in voice child responds to (often parent's voice). Keep messages short and clear. Match language to child's current level. Can use just item name ('bubbles') or full request ('I want bubbles please'). EXPANDING: One button for one item initially. Add buttons for different items in different locations. Eventually move to multi-message devices or full AAC. MULTIPLE LOCATION STRATEGY: Bubbles button in living room near bubbles. Snack button in kitchen near snacks. Outside button near door. Each button = one clear request in one clear context. WHY IT WORKS: Low motor demand – just press. Auditory feedback is reinforcing. Clear cause-effect relationship. Technology is motivating for many children.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure devices are sturdy and safe for mouthing if child explores orally. Check batteries regularly. Supervise initially to ensure appropriate use.

item.name: First Signs Visual Teaching Cards
item.description: Sign language can be a powerful requesting tool because it's always available – no device to find, no picture board needed, hands are always there. But learning signs requires imitation skills and motor planning that some children find challenging. First signs visual cards show simple signs for high-motivation items with clear photos and step-by-step hand positions. Cards for 'more,' 'eat,' 'drink,' 'help,' and specific preferred items give visual models for both child and caregivers to reference. The cards help caregivers learn the signs so they can model consistently, and provide visual supports for teaching. For children who can imitate motor movements, signs can become faster than pictures – hands are always available, always ready to communicate.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating and using first signs cards: SELECTING FIRST SIGNS: Choose signs for high-motivation items. Prioritize: 'more' (universal – works for any repeated item), 'eat/food,' 'drink,' 'help,' 'all done/finished,' Specific favorites: 'bubbles,' 'ball,' 'music,' etc. Start with 3-5 most functional signs. CARD FORMAT: Photo of the sign (final hand position). Sequence photos if sign has movement. Written description of how to make sign. Picture of item the sign represents. Consider video QR codes linking to sign demonstrations. SIGN MODIFICATIONS: Official ASL/ISL signs can be modified for motor abilities. 'Approximations' are acceptable – communication matters more than perfection. Consistent approximation within your family is fine. TEACHING APPROACH: Model the sign every time you give the item. Hand-over-hand prompting to form sign, then give item. Fade prompting progressively. Accept approximations and shape over time. FAMILY CONSISTENCY: Everyone in household should learn the signs. Post cards where relevant (kitchen, play area). Practice together so child sees signs frequently. ADVANTAGES OF SIGNS: Always available – no device to find. Fast once learned. Can combine with speech. Develops motor planning skills. CHALLENGES TO ANTICIPATE: Requires motor imitation ability. Some children struggle with fine motor control. Signs may not be understood by unfamiliar people. May work best as supplement to other systems.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Accept and reinforce approximations of signs. Demanding perfection before reinforcement kills motivation. The goal is communication, not perfect signing.

item.name: Request Communication Book (PECS-Style)
item.description: A request communication book is a portable system that grows with your child's communication. Unlike a simple board with a few pictures, a communication book can hold dozens or hundreds of request options organized by category, travel anywhere your child goes, and support increasingly complex communication. The book typically starts with a front page for active requesting, with pictures stored in categorized pages inside. The child takes a picture from inside the book, brings it to someone, and exchanges it for the item. This 'exchange' element teaches the social nature of communication – you communicate TO someone. As skills grow, the book expands to include more items, action words, and eventually sentence building. This is the foundation of PECS (Picture Exchange Communication System) and similar approaches.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Building a request communication book: BOOK STRUCTURE: Small binder or sturdy folder. Velcro strip on front cover (sentence strip area for later). Velcro strip inside front cover for 'active' pictures. Category pages inside: food, toys, activities, places, people. Pictures stored on category pages, moved to front for requesting. INITIAL SETUP: Start with just a few high-motivation pictures. Don't fill book with dozens of pictures initially. Add pictures as child masters existing ones. TEACHING EXCHANGE SEQUENCE (PECS Phases adapted): Phase 1: See item → Take picture → Give to partner → Get item (physical prompting, one picture at a time). Phase 2: Increase distance – child must travel to book and/or to communication partner. Phase 3: Picture discrimination – choose correct picture from multiple options. Phase 4: Sentence building – 'I want' + item picture. Phase 5: Responding to questions – 'What do you want?' Phase 6: Commenting – moves beyond requesting. EXCHANGE IS CRITICAL: Child must give picture TO someone. Not just touch it, not just point to it. The giving teaches communication is social – directed at another person. BOOK MANAGEMENT: Keep book accessible but organized. Child should know where book is kept. Take book everywhere – it's the child's voice. Regular inventory – add new interests, remove outgrown items. CATEGORY ORGANIZATION (as book grows): Foods/Drinks, Toys/Objects, Activities, Places, People, Feelings, Actions.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: The book is the child's communication system – treat it with importance. Don't let it get lost or damaged. Ensure child knows how to find it.

item.name: Core Vocabulary Request Symbols
item.description: Most early requesting focuses on specific items – cookies, bubbles, iPad. But there's a set of 'core vocabulary' words that appear in almost all communication and are incredibly powerful for requesting: 'more,' 'want,' 'help,' 'stop,' 'go,' 'open,' 'give.' These core words combine with any item to create flexible communication. Instead of needing a picture for every specific item (which is impossible), the child learns 'want + [point to anything]' or 'more + [activity happening].' Core vocabulary symbols – whether as pictures, buttons on a device, or signs – give your child flexible communication power that transfers across items and situations. Teaching core words alongside specific item words creates a more powerful and flexible communication system.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating and teaching core vocabulary symbols: ESSENTIAL FIRST CORE WORDS: 'more' – Request continuation of any preferred activity. 'want' – Express desire for any item or activity. 'help' – Request assistance with any challenge. 'stop' / 'all done' – End any non-preferred activity. 'go' – Request to leave, to start movement. 'open' – Request opening containers, doors, packages. SYMBOL FORMAT: Consistent symbols (choose one symbol set and use it). Larger size than item-specific pictures. Prominent placement on communication board/book/device. Color-coding can help distinguish from item words. TEACHING 'MORE': Most powerful first core word. Works with any repeatable activity: more tickles, more bubbles, more snack, more swinging. Teach by: Start preferred activity → Pause → Prompt 'more' → Continue activity. Once child uses 'more' spontaneously for one activity, transfer to others. TEACHING 'WANT': More specific than 'more' – indicates desire for item. Teach with visible desired items. 'Want' + point to item = get item. On device/board: 'want' button + item picture. TEACHING 'HELP': Introduce during tasks child can't do alone. Opening containers, reaching high items, fixing broken toys. 'Help' = immediate assistance. COMBINING CORE + SPECIFIC: 'More' + 'bubbles' = more specific request. 'Want' + 'cookie' = clear item request. 'Help' + 'open' = action request. This combination is early sentence building. PLACEMENT: Core words always visible and accessible. Don't hide them in book pages. Front of communication board/book, home page of device.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Core words should be taught to empowerment, not compliance. 'Stop' and 'no' are important core words – children need ways to refuse and protest.

item.name: AAC Requesting App (Tablet-Based)
item.description: For children who respond well to technology, AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) apps on tablets provide sophisticated requesting tools that can grow from simple single-item requests to complex sentences. Apps like TouchChat, LAMP Words for Life, Proloquo2Go, Avaz, or free options like CBoard offer organized vocabulary, voice output, and customization for your child's specific needs and interests. The tablet speaks for your child – they touch a symbol, the device says the word. Beyond simple requesting, these apps can eventually support commenting, asking questions, expressing feelings, and full conversation. Starting with requesting functions (highly motivating) builds familiarity with the device that enables growth into broader communication.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Setting up AAC for requesting: APP OPTIONS: Free/Low cost: CBoard, LetMeTalk, JABtalk. Mid-range: Avaz (₹2,000-3,000), Predictable. Premium: TouchChat, Proloquo2Go, LAMP Words for Life (₹15,000-20,000). Consider: Symbol set preference, customization options, voice options, growth potential. INITIAL SETUP FOR REQUESTING: Don't start with full vocabulary – too overwhelming. Create or use a 'requesting starter page.' Include only high-motivation items initially. Keep visible options limited (start with 4-9 items). Add 'more,' 'want,' 'help' as core words. CUSTOMIZATION: Add photos of YOUR items if possible. Record familiar voice (parent) for some apps. Organize by category as vocabulary grows. Adjust button size for motor abilities. TEACHING: Same principles as other requesting methods: Motivation (have something they want), Prompt (help them touch the symbol), Immediate reinforcement (give the item right away), Fade prompting (reduce help over time). DEVICE ACCESS: Tablet should be available for communication, not hidden. Consider dedicated communication device vs. shared family tablet. Protected case for durability. Charging routine so it's always ready. COMMON MISTAKES: Too much vocabulary too fast. Using for entertainment instead of communication. Not responding consistently when child uses it. Taking device away as 'consequence' (never do this – it's their voice). PROFESSIONAL SUPPORT: Consider consultation with SLP experienced in AAC. Many apps have training resources and communities.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: An AAC device is a communication system, not a toy or entertainment device. Never take it away as punishment – that's taking away voice. Keep it accessible always.

item.name: Environmental Arrangement Materials
item.description: Teaching requesting requires motivation, and motivation requires wanting something you don't have. Environmental arrangement means strategically organizing the environment so that desired items are visible but not accessible – creating natural opportunities to request. Clear containers show what's inside but require help to open. Shelves place favorite items in sight but out of reach. Portions are given in small amounts requiring 'more.' Preferred activities need adult help to start (iPad turned off, bubbles closed, swing needs pushing). This isn't deprivation – it's engineering communication opportunities into everyday life. Instead of meeting needs before they're expressed, environmental arrangement pauses to create space for communication.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Arranging environment for communication opportunities: VISIBILITY PRINCIPLE: Desired items should be VISIBLE but not ACCESSIBLE. Child can SEE what they want. Child needs to COMMUNICATE to get it. Builds motivation – can't get unless they ask. STRATEGIES: Clear containers: Store snacks, toys in clear containers they can't open. High shelves: Place favorites at visible but unreachable height. Closed containers: Bubble bottles closed, playdough sealed, activities packaged. Small portions: Give tiny portion of snack – creates need to request 'more.' Pause in activities: Stop preferred activity – wait for 'more' request. Needed help: Activities that require adult to start (iPad locked, swing needs push). Missing pieces: Give puzzle without pieces, give coloring without crayons, etc. LOCATION STRATEGIES: Kitchen: Snacks visible in clear containers on counter. Living room: Favorite toys on high shelf with clear view. Throughout house: Pictures/symbols for requesting at point of need. TIMING: Balance between creating opportunity and creating frustration. If child is getting too frustrated, reduce challenge. Goal is successful communication, not upset child. FAMILY TRAINING: Everyone in household needs to understand system. Natural impulse is to meet needs immediately. Explain: We're creating opportunities to communicate. We're not withholding – we're waiting for communication. MAINTAINING MOTIVATION: Rotate items so they stay desirable. Don't restrict to the point of satiation (they no longer care). Always respond when they DO communicate.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: This is about creating opportunity, not deprivation. Child's basic needs (food, water, comfort) should be met. Communication opportunity is for preferences, not necessities.

item.name: Requesting Teaching Data Sheets
item.description: Teaching requesting is a process that takes time and requires tracking to know what's working. Data sheets document which items your child requests, what communication method they use, how much prompting they need, and progress over time. Without data, you're guessing. Is the child making progress? Is this item still motivating? Is prompting being faded appropriately? Which communication method works best? Data reveals patterns that feelings can't track accurately. Even simple data – tallying successful requests, noting prompt level, tracking new vocabulary – guides teaching decisions. You can see growth that might not be obvious day-to-day, and identify problems before they become entrenched.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Creating requesting data system: SIMPLE DAILY LOG: Date. Item requested. Communication method used (picture, sign, word, device). Prompt level (full physical, partial, gestural, independent). Success? (Got the item after communicating?). Notes. PROMPT LEVEL TRACKING: Full physical: Hand-over-hand guidance to communicate. Partial physical: Touch prompt to start movement. Gestural: Pointing to communication tool. Verbal: 'What do you want?' or 'Tell me.' Independent: Child initiates without any prompt. Track prompt level for each request. Goal: Movement toward independence over time. WEEKLY SUMMARY: Total requests made. Most requested items. Communication methods used (percentage). Average prompt level. New vocabulary used. Observations and notes. PROGRESS INDICATORS: More total requests = more communication. Lower prompt levels = more independence. More varied vocabulary = broader communication. More communication partners = generalization. Longer sentences = complexity growth. DATA PATTERNS TO WATCH: Stuck at same prompt level for weeks → Change approach. Certain items never requested → Check motivation. Only requests from one person → Work on generalization. Requests only in one location → Expand contexts. FAMILY INVOLVEMENT: Keep data simple enough that everyone can contribute. Post summary where family can see progress. Celebrate milestones together.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Data should inform teaching, not create stress. If tracking is too burdensome, simplify. Even rough tallies are better than no tracking.

socialMedia.caption: Your child has wants and needs but no way to tell you. The frustration – theirs and yours – is overwhelming. But requesting can be taught. It starts with finding what motivates your child intensely, providing an accessible way to communicate (pictures, signs, buttons, apps – whatever works), and responding immediately when they try to ask. These 9 materials build the foundation: motivation items, visual boards, voice output devices, sign language cards, communication books, core vocabulary, AAC apps, environmental arrangement, and data tracking. Requesting is the most powerful communication skill because it immediately benefits your child – they communicate, they get what they want. That's the loop that builds a voice. 💬🌟 #TeachingCommunication #RequestingSkills #PinnacleBlooms
